coredata.dc:title: Na2MnPO4F/Ti3C2-CQDs composite cathode material with enhanced electrochemical properties for lithium/sodium hybrid ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Dan
Guo, Rongting
Lv, Yiju
Li, Wei
Lu, Mingjun
Wei, Qiumei
Liu, Zheng
Han, Guo-Cheng
coredata.dc:description: Na2MnPO4F and Ti3C2‑carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposites were prepared by wet mixing method combined with spray drying method, and the two materials were further compounded by heat treatment to get different ratios of Na2MnPO4F/Ti3C2-CQDs cathode material (NTC), the effects of Ti3C2-CQDs on the structure and electrochemical performance of the composite of Na2MnPO4F as a lithium-ion battery cathode material were investigated. The results showed that Na2MnPO4F was uniformly attached to the surface and between layers of Ti3C2-CQDs. The initial specific discharge capacity of NTC-2 at 0.1C was 67.8 mAh·g−1, and the reversible capacity after 100 cycles was 60.7 mAh·g−1, the capacity retention rate was as high as 90%, which all showed that Ti3C2-CQDs can enhance the cycle performance, rate performance and capacity retention rate of Na2MnPO4F. It also showed good rate performance under high current density. After the high current test, the current returned to 0.1C, and the capacity can be restored to the original value, which showed that the material has good structural stability. Therefore, Ti3C2-CQDs doped Na2MnPO4F is an important way to improve the electrochemical performance of polyanionic compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium/sodium hybrid ion battery
Cathode material
Sodium manganese fluorophosphate
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Ti3C2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ti3C2 and Ti3C2-CQDs composite materials
Synthesis of Na2MnPO4F and Na2MnPO4F/Ti3C2-CQDs cathode materials
Synthesis and structure of TC-CQDs nanocomposites
Synthesis and structure of NTC nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of Ti3AlC2 (a) and Ti3C2 (d); TEM images of CQDs (b), Ti3C2 (c) and TC-CQDs (e, f).
XRD (a) and Raman (b) pattern of Ti3C2 and TC-CQDs; XPS spectra of TC-CQDs (c-f).
XRD of NMPF material (a); SEM images (b-f) of NMPF material with different magnification: (b) 1.0 k; (c) 2.0 k; (d) 10 k; (e) 30 k and (f) 60 k.
SEM images of NTC-1(a, b), NTC-2(c, d), NTC-3(e, f).
Cycling performances of NMPF and NTC at 0.1C.

Electrochemical reaction kinetics characterization (a) Initial charge-discharge curves of NMPF and NTC; (b) Rate capability of NMPF and NTC; (c) Impedance spectra and corresponding equivalent circuit diagrams of NMPF and NTC; (d) Cyclic voltammetry of NTC-2.
Impedance parameters of NMPF and NTC.

head.ce:title: Na2MnPO4F/Ti3C2-CQDs composite cathode material with enhanced electrochemical properties for lithium/sodium hybrid ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, Guilin 541004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, 541004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Na2MnPO4F and Ti3C2‑carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposites were prepared by wet mixing method combined with spray drying method, and the two materials were further compounded by heat treatment to get different ratios of Na2MnPO4F/Ti3C2-CQDs cathode material (NTC), the effects of Ti3C2-CQDs on the structure and electrochemical performance of the composite of Na2MnPO4F as a lithium-ion battery cathode material were investigated. The results showed that Na2MnPO4F was uniformly attached to the surface and between layers of Ti3C2-CQDs. The initial specific discharge capacity of NTC-2 at 0.1C was 67.8 mAh·g−1, and the reversible capacity after 100 cycles was 60.7 mAh·g−1, the capacity retention rate was as high as 90%, which all showed that Ti3C2-CQDs can enhance the cycle performance, rate performance and capacity retention rate of Na2MnPO4F. It also showed good rate performance under high current density. After the high current test, the current returned to 0.1C, and the capacity can be restored to the original value, which showed that the material has good structural stability. Therefore, Ti3C2-CQDs doped Na2MnPO4F is an important way to improve the electrochemical performance of polyanionic compounds.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing energy crisis and environmental pollution, the search for green and renewable energy and new energy storage technology has become an urgent task in the world [1–3]. Lithium-ion batteries (LIB) stand out among many high-tech products with the advantages of green environmental protection, high energy density, high capacity, and long life [4,5]. However, the research on lithium-ion batteries has almost reached the limit and shows the characteristics of high cost. And the development of lithium-ion battery cathode materials with high specific capacity is a problem that researchers have been working on, which raises the problem of further development of so-called post‑lithium-ion batteries based on similar technologies [6–8]. Sodium ions have similar physical and chemical properties to lithium and have a high content in the earth's crust, making them an ideal substitute for lithium ions. However, in the sodium ion batteries (SIB), it is found that due to the large radius of sodium ions, the resistance to intercalation/deintercalation between the positive and negative electrodes is large; the reversibility is poor, and the irreversible capacity loss is large; the safety and stability are poor, and the electrochemical performance is low. The electrochemical performances of SIB are still a certain gap compare to LIB, and there is still a big gap to be commercial applied [9,10]. Compared with Li+, the larger ionic radius of Na+ exhibits a weaker polarization effect and forms a larger bond ion characteristic. There is a lower diffusion barrier in a specific type of ion structure between Li+ and Na+. Therefore, the sodium-based materials are introduced into lithium-ion batteries, and lithium/sodium mixed ion batteries have also emerged [11–13]. The emergence of lithium/sodium mixed ion batteries combines the high energy density and safety of lithium-ion batteries and low cost of sodium-ion batteries, changing the traditional concept that the cathode material of lithium-ion batteries must be lithium-based materials, which greatly saves lithium resource utilization. Among many materials, sodium-based polyanionic compounds have a stable structural framework, easily adjustable discharge potential platform, high electrochemical potential, and excellent safety that have attracted the attention of researchers [14–17]. However, polyanionic cathode materials have the problems of relatively low electronic conductivity and poor high-current discharge performance. Therefore, the materials need to be coated with carbon, surface coating, particle size reduction, doping, etc. [18,19]. To improve its conductivity, so that it can reach a practical level.

Among many polyanionic cathode materials, manganese-based electrode materials have attracted more and more attention due to their low cost, non-toxic, high voltage, and abundant valence [20]. Among them, Na2MnPO4F has been proven to be a very promising cathode material for lithium/sodium mixed ion batteries because of its high theoretical specific capacity (134 mAh·g−1) and high working voltage (3.6 V) [21,22]. Unfortunately, its further development is limited by the low electronic conductivity and slow ion diffusion rate [23]. To overcome these shortcomings, Zhong et al. [22] used a combination of wet ball milling and in-situ pyrolytic carbon coating to prepare Na2MnPO4F/C composites coated with different carbon sources for lithium/sodium mixed ion battery cathode material. The current first specific discharge capacity can reach 80 mAh·g−1; Xie et al. [24] used the sol-gel method to synthesize the secondary ion-doped Na2Fe1-xMnxPO4F/C composite material, which was applied to the cathode material of lithium/sodium mixed ion battery, and the initial specific discharge capacity of the prepared Na2Fe0.5Mn0.5PO4F at 12.4 mA·g−1 was 107 mAh·g−1. Wu et al. [25] synthesized hollow spherical carbon-coated Na2MnPO4F/C nanocomposites by spray drying and in-situ pyrolysis, which was used as cathode material of lithium/sodium mixed ion battery, and the first charge-discharge specific capacity reached 102.3 mAh·g−1 at 0.05C. It can be seen that the electrochemical performance of Na2MnPO4F is effectively improved by reducing the particle size, doping carbon coating, secondary ions, etc., so that its first-turn discharge specific capacity at low current density is significantly increased, but its cycle stability, discharge specific capacity under high current density and capacity retention rate still need to be improved. This may be due to the high-temperature sintering during the synthesis process to agglomerate particles. Therefore, it is still necessary to design a new strategy for Na2MnPO4F to reduce particle agglomeration, improve specific discharge capacity and cycle stability.

As a new type of two-dimensional material, Ti3C2 have attracted much attention because of their high specific surface area, good electrical conductivity, excellent mechanical properties, and electrochemical properties similar to graphene [26–28]. Researchers have found that it has excellent lithium storage performance [29]. In addition, its large specific surface area and layered structure can serve as a short path for lithium/sodium ions and electrons to diffuse in the composite cathode, thereby forming a conductive network, and at the same time improving the electronic and ionic conductivity. Therefore, it can be expected that the use of Ti3C2 to modify Na2MnPO4F nanoparticles can shorten the diffusion distance of Li+ to form a conductive network, thereby achieving the purpose of improving the electrochemical performance of Na2MnPO4F.

In this work, Ti3C2-CQDs composite material was prepared by wet mixing method, hollow spherical Na2MnPO4F micro-nano material was prepared by spray drying method, and heat treatment was used to combine the two to construct a conductive network to get different ratios of Na2MnPO4F/Ti3C2-CQDs cathode material (NTC-1, NTC-2, NTC-3). They were used as LIB cathode materials to study the electrochemical performance. It was proved through research that CQDs improved the easy collapse problem of Ti3C2, thereby improving the interlayer spacing, and cooperate with Ti3C2 to improve the structural stability and electrochemical performance of Na2MnPO4F, so as to improve the electronic conductivity of Na2MnPO4F.

Titanium aluminum carbide powder (Ti3AlC2) was procured from Forsman Scientific(Beijing)Co.,Ltd., Hydrofluoric acid (HF), Hydrogen peroxide30% (30 % H2O2), Acetic acid (CH3COOH), Sodium acetate trihydrate (CH3COONa·3H2O), Ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4) and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone were procured from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd., Manganese acetate tetrahydrate (Mn(CH3COO)2·4H2O) was procured from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., Sodium fluoride (NaF) and poly(vinylidene fluoride) were procured from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., acetylene black was procured from Taiyuan Liyuan Lithium Battery Technology Center Co., Ltd. All the precursors were used directly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ti3C2 and Ti3C2-CQDs composite materials
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Ti3C2 was carried out according to the literature [30]. Under magnetic stirring, 1 g Ti3AlC2 was slowly added to 20 mL HF and reacted at 35 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the product was centrifuged and washed with ultrapure water several times until the pH of the filtrate was greater than 6. The precipitate was collected and dried under vacuum to obtain a multilayer Ti3C2 powder, ready for use.

The synthesis of CQDs was carried out according to the literature [31,32]. The bagasse powder (which had been dried and crushed) was calcined at 700 °C for 2 h to obtain the bagasse carbon material. 2 g bagasse carbon material was weighed and dispersed uniformly in 500 mL 30 % H2O2 and 240 mL CH3COOH, ultrasonicated for 30 min, stirred and refluxed at 100 °C for 12 h, cooled to room temperature, filtered to take the liquid and spin-evaporate to 5–10 mL, dialyzed for 24 h to obtain CQDs are diluted to 100 mL and set aside. Concentration measurement:Based on the weight difference before and after freeze drying, 100 mL CQDs solution was measured, and the concentration of CQDs was 1.738 mg/mL according to formula (1). The study found that the low concentration of quantum dots has little effect on the interlayer support of Ti3C2, and too high concentration may hinder the penetration of electrolyte and the transmission of electrons and ions, so the concentration of CQDs we added is 1.738 mg/mL.
(1)
C CQDs
=
m Beaker quality after lyophilization
−
m Empty beaker mass
V CQDs
× 1000

The Ti3C2 was weighed out 0.1677 g, dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, mixed thoroughly and sonicated for 30 min. After sonication, the Ti3C2 suspension was obtained. After stirring for another 2 h, 100 mL of CQDs were added and stirred for 6 h and then placed in a blast drying oven for 12 h. The drying temperature was 65 °C. The material was taken out, and the Ti3C2-CQDs composite material (hereinafter referred to as TC-CQDs) was obtained after grinding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Na2MnPO4F and Na2MnPO4F/Ti3C2-CQDs cathode materials
ce:section.ce:para: According to the stoichiometric ratio [25], 4.9018 g of Mn(CH3COO)2·4H2O, 2.7216 g of CH3COONa·3H2O, 0.8398 g of NaF, and 2.3004 g of NH4H2PO4 were weighed, respectively; 20 mL of pure water was added, stirred at 80 °C for 8 h, and spray dried to obtain Na2MnPO4F precursor. The Na2MnPO4F was calcined at 300 °C for 3 h in a tubular furnace filled with Ar atmosphere and then calcined at 600 °C for 6 h. After the tube furnace was cooled to room temperature, Na2MnPO4F can be obtained (hereinafter referred to as NMPF).

The NMPF and TC-CQDs powders were weighed according to the mass ratio in Table 1 , dispersed in an appropriate amount of ethanol solvent, ultrasonicated for 0.5 h, stirred for 2 h, mixed and stirred for 6 h, air-dried, ground and filled with Ar calcined at 400 °C for 2 h in a tube furnace. After cooling to room temperature, NTC-1, NTC-2 and NTC-3 composites were obtained.

X'Pert3 Powder type multifunctional X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrument (Panaco, Netherlands, Cu target, λ = 1.54056 Å) was used to analyze the crystal structure of the sample. The test scan range was 5–90°, and the scan speed was 0.6565°/s. The field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) produced by Hitachi, Japan was used to systematically observe the microscopic morphology and particle size of the material. The elemental composition and distribution of the samples were determined by Energy Dispersive Spectrometer in Germany. The FEI Tecnai G2 F20 high-magnification transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was used to analyze the particle size, structure, and lattice fringes of the material. The InYia laser Raman spectrometer (Raman) of the British Renishaw Company was used to judge the degree of carbon graphitization. The Thermo fisher Scientific K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was used to test the element composition and chemical valence of materials.

The electrochemical performance of the NTC composite material was measured in a CR2032 button half-cell, with metallic lithium as the negative electrode and polypropylene porous membrane as the separator. The electrode material was made by mixing 70 wt% of active material, 15 wt% acetylene black, 15 wt% poly(vinylidene fluoride), and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone solvent. The slurry was coated on aluminum foil and kept at 80 °C vacuum drying. The mass loading of the electrode material was calculated as 0.59 mg·cm2. 1.0 mol/L LiPF6 ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate (1/1, v/v) mixture was used as the electrolyte, and the battery was assembled in an Ar protected glove box (both oxygen and water vapor content are less than 1 ppm). The constant current charge/discharge of the battery was measured in the voltage range of 1.5 to 4.1 V using the Xinwei battery test system. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was tested on an electrochemical workstation (CHI860D, Shanghai, China) at different scan rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structure of TC-CQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The raw material Ti3AlC2 and the Ti3C2 samples prepared by the method in Section 2.2 were tested and analyzed by scanning electron microscopy. The results are shown in Fig. 1 (a, d). It can be seen from Fig. 1(a) that the Ti3AlC2 presents a densely stacked layered structure. After HF etching, the Al layer in Ti3AlC2 is etched away, and the morphology of the material changes significantly. The layer spacing increases, showing the obvious accordion shape in Fig. 1(d) [33]. It is confirmed that the method of HF etching Ti3AlC2 to obtain Ti3C2 is effective, and it also shows that the prepared material is indeed Ti3C2 material [34]. The CQDs, Ti3C2, and TC-CQDs composite materials prepared by the method in Section 2.2 are analyzed by TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 (b, c, e, f). From Fig. 1(b), it can be seen that CQDs have obvious boundaries. The spherical morphology is basically monodisperse, the particle diameter is between 5 and 10 nm, the size distribution is uniform and the dispersion is good, and it is confirmed to be CQDs [35–37]. Comparing Fig. 1(c) and Fig. 1(e, f), it can be seen that the edges of the Ti3C2 nano-layer in the TC-CQDs material are modified by many CQDs nanoparticles, which can prevent the Ti3C2 nano-layer from collapsing, re-stacking.

The raw material Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2 and TC-CQDs in Section 2.2 were subjected to XRD test and analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 2(a). The diffraction peaks of Ti3AlC2 in Fig. 2(a) correspond to its standard card PDF (NO.52–0875). After selective etching, the diffraction peaks of Ti3C2 samples obtained are consistent with those reported in the literature [38]. After doping with CQDs, the intensities of the characteristic peaks (004), (006), (008), (010) and (110) of Ti3C2 are significantly reduced, while the diffraction peaks appearing near 20° are slightly enhanced, which indicates that CQDs are successfully doped. And no other substances are incorporated, and CQDs are attached between the Ti3C2 layers and on the surface, which weakens the characteristic peak intensity. The Raman test analysis results of Ti3C2 and TC-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2(b). The peaks in the figure are attributed to the vibration modes of rutile and anatase [39], the vibrational peak of the C atom, and the TiC bonding in Ti3C2 [40]. The 1352 and 1585 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of TC-CQDs correspond to the disordered carbon and ordered graphitic carbon in CQDs [41].

The chemical composition of TC-CQDs was further explored by the XPS test. Fig. 2(c) confirmed the presence of four elements C, Ti, O, and F in the sample, and no Al element was detected. This means that Al has been almost completely removed during the etching process. In Fig. 2(d), the C1s spectrum contains two peaks at 282.0 and 284.8 eV, corresponding to C-Ti-TX (T = -O, F, -OH) and CC. In Fig. 2(e), the peaks of Ti 2p high-resolution spectra at 454.8, 455.5, 456, 459, 461, 462 eV correspond to TiC 2p3/2, Ti2+ 2p3/2, Ti3+ 2p3/2, TiC 2p1/2, Ti2+ 2p1/2 and Ti3+ 2p1/2 respectively [42,43]. The TiF and AlF peaks corresponding to the F1s spectrum in Fig. 2(f) indicate the presence of F and trace Al elements in the sample [44,45]. Therefore, Ti3C2 and CQDs form a mixed composite structure, which is consistent with XRD analysis.

The XRD pattern of the NMPF sample is shown in Fig. 3(a). All XRD patterns in the figure can be well indexed as Na2MnPO4F monoclinic structure with space group P21/n, and are consistent with the Na2MnPO4F standard card PDF (NO.87–0467). There is no impurity phase, indicating that the sample is indeed Na2MnPO4F and the purity is well [23,25,46]. The XRD pattern of the sample is refined by Rietveld through Jade software, as shown in Fig. 3(b). The refined lattice parameters are: a = 13.729 Å, b = 5.334(7)Å, c = 13.739(5)Å, β = 119.775, which are very close to the previously reported values [47].The morphology of the sample was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM images at different magnifications are shown in Fig. 3(c-f). It can be seen that the particle morphology of the NMPF sample is approximately spherical, the crystal form is round and full, and the primary NMPF sample nanoparticles have a small size (tens of nanometers), which is conducive to the transport of Li+, Na+ and electrons [48]. From Fig. 3(e, f), it can be observed that the secondary structure of micrometer size is formed by the aggregation of primary nanoparticles, and presents the structure of hollow spheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structure of NTC nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns of the NTC composites with different ratios prepared in Section 2.3. It can be seen from the figure that the diffraction peak of the composite material corresponds to the single structure of NMPF and TC-CQDs. No impurity phase is detected, which confirms that the purity of the composite material is relatively high. With the increase in the amount of TC-CQDs added, the Na2MnPO4F diffraction peak intensity gradually decreases. Further SEM analysis of the NTC composites, the results are shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen from the figure that the layered TC-CQDs are uniformly dispersed in the NMPF, and the lamellar structure is obviously distributed. The content of TC-CQDs in Fig. 5 (a, b) is less, and only part of the NMPF is in contact with the TC-CQDs. Compared with Fig. 5(c- f), the content of TC-CQDs is increasing, especially in Fig. 5(f), NMPF is basically attached to TC-CQDs, which corresponds to the weakening of the diffraction peaks of NMPF in the XRD pattern of NTC-3. EDS test of NTC-2 was performed to observe the element distribution in the material. The results are shown in Fig. 6 . It can be observed that NMPF is uniformly distributed on the surface of TC-CQDs, with a few interlayers intercalated.

Fig. 7 is the cycle performance diagram of NMPF and NTC composites with different ratios at 0.1C for 100 cycles, in which the voltage range is 1.5–4.1 V. After 100 cycles of charging and discharging, the specific discharge capacity of NMPF is 3.02 mAh·g−1, and the capacity retention rate is 87 %. The specific discharge capacity of NTC-1 doped with TC-CQDs is 26.0 mAh·g−1, and the capacity retention rate is 86 %, which is significantly higher than that of pure NMPF. With the increase of TC-CQDs, the specific discharge capacity of NTC-2 is increased to 67.8 mAh·g−1, and the capacity retention rate is as high as 90 %. It can be seen that the addition of TC-CQDs greatly improve the specific discharge capacity, cycle stability and capacity retention rate of NMPF, which is attributed to the fact that the addition of TC-CQDs appropriately prevents the active material from contacting the electrolyte, thereby preventing the active material from reacting with the electrolyte, and inhibiting the dissolution of Mn3+ during the process of charge-discharge [49], which is better than the performance shown in previous reports [22,25]. With the further increase of TC-CQDs, the specific discharge capacity of NTC-3 decreased to 31.0 mAh·g−1, and the capacity retention rate remained at 90 %. This shows that adding too much TC-CQDs will hinder electrolyte penetration and ions transmission.

Fig. 8(a) shows the first charge-discharge curve of NMPF and NTC composites with different ratios. It can be observed from the figure that the first charge-discharge curve of NMPF does not show a plateau, showing that NMPF has almost no electrochemical activity, which corresponds to the extremely low specific discharge capacity in its cycle performance graph [15,21]. In contrast, the charge and discharge curves of NTC vary greatly, and there are different degrees of polarization. Among them, the charge and discharge curves of NTC-1 and NTC-3 both appear to have a plateau at about 2.0 V. NTC-2 has an obvious plateau at about 2.5 V, which can be attributed to the fact that the addition of TC-CQDs in the lamellae increases the electronic conductivity and ion diffusion rate, improving the electrochemical activity of NMPF and alleviating the Mn3+ of Jahn-Teller distortion reduces the polarization effect [50].

NMPF and different ratios of NTC composites were tested for rate performance under different charge and discharge currents, and the results are shown in Fig. 8(b). It can be seen from the figure that when the current density increases from 0.1C to 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5C, the specific discharge capacity of NMPF changes very little and still maintains an extremely low state. This is related to the low intrinsic electronic and ion conductivity and poor electrochemical activity of NMPF [51,52]. As the current density increases, the specific discharge capacity of NTC with different ratios shows a decreasing trend. It can be seen that the capacity of NTC decays faster when the current density increases. When the current density increases from 0.1C to 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5C, the specific discharge capacities of NTC-2 are 82.9, 54.1, 44.6, 34.7, 25.9 and 12.8 mAh·g−1, respectively. After the high current test, the current returns to 0.1C, and the capacity can be restored to the original value, indicating that the material has good structural stability.

To further study the electrochemical kinetics, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the NMPF and NTC electrodes in the first fully discharged state was studied. The result is shown in Fig. 8(c), the equivalent circuit diagram is attached to Fig. 8(c), and the impedance parameter values after fitting are listed in Table 2 . It can be observed from the figure that the nyquist curves of the NMPF and NTC electrodes are both semicircular in the high-frequency region and oblique lines in the low-frequency region. Among them, the semicircle corresponds to the charge transfer resistance (R ct) of the electrode material, and the diagonal line is related to the ion diffusion process in the electrode material [53]. It can be seen from the figure that the high-frequency region of the NTC-2 electrode is a smaller semicircle, and the slope of the low-frequency region is larger, indicating that the NTC-2 electrode has a low charge transfer resistance and a low ion diffusion resistance [54]. From the equivalent circuit diagram to further simulate the kinetic results of NMPF and NTC electrodes, it can also be seen that NTC-2 exhibits lower R ct and faster charge transfer kinetics [25]. The result of the CV test, performed on NTC-2 at 0.1 mV·s−1, is shown in Fig. 8(d). There is a pair of oxidation/reduction peaks in the range of 1.5–4.1 V, and the oxidation/reduction peak potentials are about 3.34 and 2.97 V, respectively. This is related to the reversible transformation of Mn2+/Mn3+, accompanied by the reversible detach/embed of Na+/Li+ in the NTC-2, confirming the electrochemical behavior of NTC-2.

In this paper, a new type of TC-CQDs composite material was synthesized. Through CQDs, while improving the spacing and collapse of Ti3C2 layers, it also cooperated with Ti3C2 to modify Na2MnPO4F. By constructing a conductive layer, Na2MnPO4F was uniformly attached to the surface and layer of TC-CQDs. Thus, while reducing the diffusion path of electrons and ions, the structure was more stable, and the purpose of improving electronic conductivity, structural stability and electrochemical activity was achieved. The results showed that NTC-2 could provide a specific discharge capacity of 67.8 mAh·g−1 at 0.1C, and the reversible capacity after 100 cycles was 60.7 mAh·g−1, and the capacity retention rate at 1.5–4.1 V was as high as 90 %. The cycle stability, rate performance and capacity retention rate were significantly better than Na2MnPO4F. Therefore, our research strategy provides new ideas for Ti3C2 and CQDs in the field of lithium/sodium ion hybrid battery cathode material modification and also opens up new doors for the application of Na2MnPO4F in lithium ion battery electrode materials.
Zheng Liu, Guo-Cheng Han and Wei Li conceived and designed the experiments; Dan Sun and Qiumei Wei performed the experiments; Rongting Guo and Yiju Lv analyzed the data; Mingjun Lu wrote the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support afforded by the following organizations: the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52004076, 61661014), the Nature Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2018GXNSFBA281114, 2020GXNSFAA297054, 2018GXNSFAA281198, 2018GXNSFBA281135), and finally, Guangxi One Thousand Young and Middle-aged College and University Backbone Teachers Cultivation Program.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards a large-scale integration of renewable energies in Morocco
J.Energy Storage

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optimal scheduling for electric vehicle battery swapping-charging system based on nanogrids
